elements.sent-2411-001: The Aldous-Hoover Theorem concerns an infinite matrix of random variables whose distribution is invariant under finite permutations of rows and columns.
elements.sent-2411-002: It states that, up to equality in distribution, each random variable in the matrix can be expressed as a function only depending on four key variables: one common to the entire matrix, one that encodes information about its row, one that encodes information about its column, and a fourth one specific to the matrix entry.
elements.sent-2411-003: We state and prove the theorem within a category-theoretic approach to probability, namely the theory of Markov categories.
elements.sent-2411-004: This makes the proof more transparent and intuitive when compared to measure-theoretic ones.
elements.sent-2411-005: A key role is played by a newly identified categorical property, the Cauchy--Schwarz axiom, which also facilitates a new synthetic de Finetti Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-006: We further provide a variant of our proof using the ordered Markov property and the d-separation criterion, both generalized from Bayesian networks to Markov categories.
elements.sent-2411-007: We expect that this approach will facilitate a systematic development of more complex results in the future, such as categorical approaches to hierarchical exchangeability.
elements.sent-2411-008: The celebrated de Finetti Theorem is concerned with \newterm{exchangeable probability measures $p$ on a countably infinite power $X^\N$ of a sample space $X$, where exchangeability means that $p$ is invariant under finite permutations of the factors.}
elements.sent-2411-009: The theorem states that every such $p$ can be written as a mixture of iid measures.
elements.sent-2411-010: More precisely, for every exchangeable $p$ there exist a measure $q$ on some other space $A$ and a Markov kernel $f \colon A \to X$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:definetti_intro} \tikzfig{de_finetti_standard}\end{equation} This equation is written in the string-diagrammatic notation of categorical probability.\footnotemark \footnotetext{The objects $X^1, X^2, \dots$ on the right-hand side are all equal to $X$, but we use labels in superscripts to indicate how these match up with the factors of the object $X^\N = \bigotimes_{n \in \N X$ on the left-hand side.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: This notation will become more important in the two-dimensional case of the Aldous--Hoover Theorem discussed below.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, we omit the superscripts in other cases when the matching of outputs is unambiguous.
elements.sent-2411-013: It can be read as follows: Sampling a random sequence $(x_n) \in X^\N$ from $p$ can be achieved by first sampling $a \in A$ from $q$ and then, for each $n \in \N$, sampling $x_n \in X$ from the probability measure $f( \ph | a)$.
elements.sent-2411-014: This recovers the traditional formulation of the de Finetti theorem in terms of conditional iid sequences of random variables \cite[Theorem 1.1]{kallenberg2005symmetries .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This result is among the most foundational theorems in probability theory with substantial technical and philosophical significance.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, it plays central roles in Bayesian non-parametric statistics and in the longstanding debate on the subjective vs.\ objective view of probability \cite{oneill2009exchangeability .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In an earlier work \cite{fritz2021definetti , the above formulation of the de Finetti Theorem is stated and proven in a purely synthetic fashion.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This means that neither the statement nor the proof uses measure theory.
elements.sent-2411-019: Instead, both live entirely in the categorical framework of probability theory based on \newterm{Markov categories \cite{chojacobs2019strings,fritz2019synthetic , which axiomatizes how probabilities behave instead of defining what probabilities are.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: This formalism also can be instantiated on other theories of uncertainty \cite{fritz2024causal .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, its string-diagrammatic language provides an intuitive account of probabilistic processes and the (in)dependencies present.
elements.sent-2411-022: Using extra axioms with a probabilistic interpretation (e.g.\ conditionals, representability, and others) on top of the definition of Markov category gives us the structure needed to express and prove categorical versions of classical results in probability theory and statistics \cite{fritzrischel2019zeroone, fritz2023representable, fritz2021definetti, fritz2022dseparation, fritz2024hidden,moss2022ergodic,ackerman2024randomgraphs,dilavore2023evidential,pvb2024martingales .}
elements.sent-2411-023: For example, the synthetic version of the de Finetti Theorem recovers the traditional statement when instantiated in the Markov category $\bm{\BorelStoch}$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Moreover, its application to other Markov categories yields new concrete versions of the theorem \cite[Corollary 5.47]{forre2021quasimeasurable .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The aim of this paper is to formulate and prove a synthetic version of the \newterm{Aldous--Hoover Theorem .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Its classical version \cite{aldous1981representations,aldous1985exchangeability,hoover1982row achieves a similar feat as the de Finetti Theorem, but for an infinite matrix of random variables rather than a sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Exchangeability of the sequence is replaced by \newterm{row-column exchangeability (also known as separate exchangeability), which means that the distribution is invariant under finite permutations of its rows and columns of the matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Using this assumption, the theorem concludes a plethora of conditional independence relations.
elements.sent-2411-029: These imply that the joint distribution can be decomposed in a way that is analogous to the de Finetti Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-030: Before presenting the decomposition abstractly, let us offer an intuitive explanation.
elements.sent-2411-031: Imagine two countably infinite groups of chess players consisting of Americans and Europeans respectively.
elements.sent-2411-032: They play a round-robin tournament, where each American plays against each European exactly once, so that the outcomes can be listed as in \cref{fig:rough_ah .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Suppose that we are interested in generating samples of what such a table might look like, provided that the only thing we know about each player is their membership in the respective team.
elements.sent-2411-034: So we intend to sample from a probability distribution over outcome tables which is invariant under permuting a finite number of Americans (or of Europeans), and hence row-column exchangeable.
elements.sent-2411-035: In such a situation, the Aldous--Hoover Theorem says the following: One can randomly associate a number{\,---\, let us call it the asymptotic Elo rating{\,---\, to each player, such that the outcome of each game depends (probabilistically) only on the asymptotic Elo ratings of the two players together with possible external factors (e.g.\ location of the tournament, weather, etc.).}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Moreover, according to the theorem, the asymptotic Elo ratings similarly depend on the external factors only (possibly in a different way for each team).
elements.sent-2411-037: Turning to the formal statement, our \newterm{Synthetic Aldous--Hoover Theorem (\cref{thm:AldousHooverWeak ) extends the de Finetti Theorem expressed via \cref{eq:definetti_intro as follows.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: In a Markov category with conditionals, countable Kolmogorov products and satisfying the Cauchy--Schwarz axiom,\footnotemark{ \footnotetext{See \cref{sec:prelims for more detail on these assumptions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In the main text, we avoid such complicated string diagrams and use the \newterm{plate notation instead (\cref{sec:plate_notation ). \begin{equation}\label{eq:aldous_hoover_intro} \tikzfig{aldous_hoover_standard}\end{equation} for suitable morphisms $q$, $f$, $g$, and $h$.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: In our chess tournament example, each $X^{i,j}$ stands for the sample space $\{A,D,E\}$ of the possible results of the game of the $i$-th European against the $j$-th American.
elements.sent-2411-041: The morphism $q$ gives a distribution over the possible values of external factors, while $f$ and $g$ simulate the asymptotic Elo ratings of Americans and Europeans respectively as random functions of external factors.
elements.sent-2411-042: The simulation of the result of an individual game is performed by $h$ as a random function taking the two relevant asymptotic Elo ratings as well as the external factors as inputs.

elements.sent-2411-085: In essence, a \newterm{Markov category is a symmetric monoidal category $(\cC, \otimes , I)$ such that the monoidal unit $I$ is terminal, and every object $X$ comes equipped with a commutative comonoid structure in a way that is compatible with the monoidal structure.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The comonoid structure morphisms are called \newterm{copy , denoted $\cop_X \colon X \to X \otimes X$, and \newterm{delete , denoted $\discard_X \colon X \to I$.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: In string diagrams, we write them as \begin{equation}\tikzfig{copy_del}\end{equation} It is important to keep in mind that the copy morphisms are not natural.
elements.sent-2411-088: A morphism $f$ with respect to which copying is natural, meaning that $f$ commutes with copy, is called \newterm{deterministic .}
elements.sent-2411-089: These morphisms form a cartesian monoidal subcategory of $\cC$.
elements.sent-2411-090: Additionally, morphisms whose domain is the monoidal unit are referred to as \newterm{states .}
elements.sent-2411-091: The central example for this work is the Markov category $\BorelStoch$, whose objects are standard Borel spaces, morphisms are Markov kernels composed via the Chapman--Kolmogorov equation, and the monoidal structure is given by the usual products of measurable spaces (on objects) and Markov kernels (on morphisms).

elements.section-2411-004: The Aldous--Hoover Theorem via Markov Properties
elements.sent-2411-288: In this section, we present another proof of \cref{thm:AldousHoover by interpreting its corresponding string diagram as a Bayesian network and applying the ordered Markov property{\,---\, a criterion for when a state can be written as a Bayesian network of a given form.}}
elements.sent-2411-289: For the sake of simplicity, throughout this section we assume that $\cC$ is a strict\footnotemark{ Markov category $\cC$ with conditionals.}
elements.sent-2411-290: Every Markov category is equivalent to a strict one such that the equivalence preserves copy morphisms \cite[Theorem 10.17]{fritz2019synthetic .}
elements.sent-2411-291: Therefore, strictness is a convenient assumption we can make without loss of generality.
elements.sent-2411-292: A (causal) Bayesian network is defined via a directed acyclic graph (DAG) that encodes causal relationships.
elements.sent-2411-293: This more general approach natively accommodates hidden variables, input variables, and certain symmetries, neither of which can be encoded when using traditional Bayesian networks.
elements.sent-2411-294: We now give a brief summary of the main definitions and results from \cite{fritz2022dseparation, fritz2022free and refer the reader to these papers for a more detailed discussion.}
elements.sent-2411-295: Every DAG can also be represented as a string diagram.
elements.sent-2411-296: For example, a DAG of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:linear_model_dag} \tikzfig{linear_model_dag}\end{equation} reads as a string diagram as \begin{equation}\label{eq:linear_model_string} \varphi \quad = \quad \tikzfig{linear_model_string}\end{equation} Each node in the DAG corresponds to a wire in the string diagram, which is the output of a box whose inputs are determined by the incoming arrows of the node in the DAG.
elements.sent-2411-297: The overall output wires correspond to the observable variables while the others are latent.
elements.sent-2411-298: The boxes themselves do not represent any particular Markov kernels or physical mechanisms, but rather serve as placeholders for depicting causal relationships and their labels are formal variables.
elements.sent-2411-299: Following \cite{fritz2022dseparation , we use the term generalized causal model to refer to any such string diagram.}
elements.sent-2411-300: Formally these string diagrams serve as morphisms within a Markov category, specifically a \newterm{free Markov category ~\cite{fritz2022dseparation .}}
elements.sent-2411-301: A morphism $f$ in a Markov category $\cC$ is said to be \newterm{compatible with a generalized causal model $\varphi$ if there exists a strict Markov functor $F$ from the free Markov category containing $\varphi$ to $\cC$ such that $F(\varphi) = f$ holds, and such that $F$ maps the inputs and outputs of $\varphi$ to those of $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-302: The idea is that such $F$ assigns an object of $\cC$ to every wire in $\varphi$ and a specific morphism in $\cC$ to every box in $\varphi$.
elements.sent-2411-303: In a traditional interpretation, the object is the space of possible values of a variable associated to the wire and the morphism is a conditional probability that generates the values of its output variables given the values of its input variables.
elements.sent-2411-304: The requirement $F(\varphi) = f$ together with matching of inputs and outputs amounts to saying that the morphism $f$ can be decomposed as a string diagram whose form matches $\varphi$ (see \cref{fig:compatibility ).}
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{center} \tikzfig{compatibility_visual} \end{center} \caption{A morphism $f$ is said to be compatible with a causal model $\varphi$ if there is a strict Markov functor that maps every wire $W$ to an object $W' = F(W)$ in $\cC$ and the boxes $\alpha_i$ to morphisms in $\cC$ such that $f = F(\varphi)$.} \label{fig:compatibility}
elements.sent-2411-305: Assuming that conditionals exist, \newterm{Markov properties}\footnotemark{ provide a practical criterion for deciding whether a given morphism $f$ is compatible with a given (generalized) causal model $\varphi$, by reducing the problem to checking whether the morphism $f$ satisfies (a particular subset of) conditional independence relations derived from the model $\varphi$.}
elements.sent-2411-306: For example, a state $I \to X \otimes Y \otimes Z$ is compatible with the causal model depicted in \eqref{eq:linear_model_dag and \eqref{eq:linear_model_string if and only if it satisfies the conditional independence \begin{equation}X \perp Z \mid Y.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-307: This reflects the fact that the information flow from $X$ to $Z$ is obstructed by the box that outputs $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-308: In other words, conditioning on the value of $Y$ removes any correlation between $X$ and $Z$.
elements.sent-2411-309: There are multiple different Markov properties known (see for example \cite[Section 1]{pearl2009causality in the language of DAGs) that are necessary and sufficient for compatibility:.}
elements.sent-2411-310: The \newterm{global Markov property characterizes all conditional independences implied by the causal model in terms of a combinatorial criterion known as $d$-separation.}
elements.sent-2411-311: In the language of Markov categories, $d$-separation can be phrased as topological disconnectedness after cutting wires in the string diagram \cite{fritz2022dseparation .}
elements.sent-2411-312: The \newterm{local Markov property postulates a smaller set of conditional independences compared to the global Markov property.}
elements.sent-2411-313: It asks for one conditional independence relation of a specific type for every box, which intuitively requires the outputs of the box to be independent of ``graphical non-descendants'' given its inputs.
elements.sent-2411-314: Similarly to the local Markov property, the \newterm{ordered Markov property asks for one conditional independence relation for every box but of a different type, which is determined by a timing function.}
elements.sent-2411-315: The timing function can be interpreted as specifying the order in which outputs are generated, and the Markov property intuitively requires that, given all the inputs of a box, the outputs of the box are independent of all the other outputs generated before them or at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-316: While the first two Markov properties have already been formalized in the language of Markov categories in \cite{fritz2022dseparation , here we develop the ordered Markov property as another necessary and sufficient criterion for the compatibility with a causal model in \cref{ssec:orderedMarkov , where we also review the local Markov property.}}
elements.sent-2411-317: In \cref{ssec:Markov_AldousHoover , we then employ the ordered Markov property to provide an alternative proof of the Aldous--Hoover Theorem in the strong form of \cref{thm:AldousHoover .}}
elements.sent-2411-318: We expect this proof to facilitate a systematic study of representation theorems for hierarchical exchangeability \cite{austin2014hierarchical,lee2022deFinetti,jung2021generalization in the future.}
elements.sent-2411-319: Here, we introduce the ordered Markov property and show that it is a necessary and sufficient condition for a morphism to be compatible with a causal model.
elements.sent-2411-320: We start by introducing basic terminology for generalized causal models.
elements.sent-2411-321: For a given generalized causal model $\varphi$, we denote its set of wires by $W(\varphi)$ and its set of boxes by $B(\varphi)$.
elements.sent-2411-322: For example, the causal model in \eqref{eq:linear_model_string has \begin{equation}W(\varphi) = \{X,Y,Z\} \quad \text{ and } \quad B(\varphi) = \{\alpha, \beta,\gamma\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-323: For every box $b \in B(\varphi)$, we also write $\In(b)$ for the set of its input wires and $\Out(b)$ for the set of its output wires.
elements.sent-2411-324: For example, in \eqref{eq:linear_model_string , we have $\In(\beta) = \{X\}$ and $\Out(\beta) = \{Y\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-325: It is convenient to introduce an order relation, which is similar to a causal order, although it considers both wires and boxes.

elements.sent-2411-346: A \newterm{timing function for a generalized causal model $\varphi$ is a map $\timing \colon B(\varphi) \to \N$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:timing_def} b \to c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \timing(b) < \timing(c).\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-348: We think of $\timing(b) < \timing(c)$ as saying that the event $c$ happens after the event $b$, and this motivates \cref{eq:timing_def , since if $b$ causes $c$, then $c$ should happen after $b$.}
elements.sent-2411-349: While the relevant information encoded in $\timing$ is merely the induced preorder relation on boxes, we find it intuitive to introduce this in the form of a function.
elements.sent-2411-350: If a timing function is injective, then we obtain a total order on the boxes; for a string diagram obtained from a DAG, this corresponds to the standard ordering of the variables in the context of the ordered Markov property~\cite[Theorem~1.2.6]{pearl2009causality .}
